1.synthesis:
Leviticus 22 moves from the personal holiness requirements of priests (chapter 21) to their functional holiness: the conditions under which they may eat the sacred donations and the standards they must maintain when presenting offerings. The chapter's opening legislation (vv. 1–9) establishes when a priest is disqualified from eating the qodashim (holy things): any priest who approaches the holy things while ritually impure faces the karet penalty — 'that person shall be cut off from my presence: I am YHWH' (v. 3). The specific impurity conditions listed mirror those of chapters 13–15: skin disease (tzara'at), genital discharge (zov), corpse-contact, and seminal emission (vv. 4–8).

The priestly access to sacred food is framed as sustenance, not merely privilege: 'he may eat of the holy things, for they are his food' (v. 7). The Levitical system was a labor-for-donation economy: the priest serves at the altar; the people bring offerings; the priest receives portions of those offerings as his provision. The purity requirements protect both the priest and the system: a priest who eats in impurity has not merely violated a rule but has treated the holy as common and put his own life at risk.

The household boundaries for eating the priestly portions are specified in vv. 10–13: outsiders, hired workers, and tenants may not eat from the sacred donations. Permitted persons include: the priest's slaves (born in the household or purchased), his daughter if she remains unmarried in his house, and a widowed or divorced daughter who has returned to her father's household without children. A laywoman who inadvertently eats holy food must make restitution at full value plus twenty percent (v. 14). The household boundary is both protective and inclusive: it restricts access to prevent profanation, but includes all who genuinely belong to the priestly household within the covenant provision.
1.historical_context:
The terumah (priestly portion) economy is one of the more carefully legislated systems in the Torah. The boundary between those who may and may not eat from sacred donations reflects the Holiness Code's consistent concern with preserving the distinction between holy and common. The zar (outsider, stranger to the priesthood) is excluded not as an ethnic category but as a functional one: anyone not integrated into the priestly household lacks the covenantal status that makes participation in the holy food appropriate. The 20% surcharge for inadvertent eating (v. 14) is identical to the guilt offering restitution principle of Leviticus 5:16, confirming that violations of the sacred food boundary, even unintentional, carry real restitutionary weight.

The daughter's status in vv. 12–13 is instructive: if she marries a layman she loses access to the sacred portions; if she returns to her father's house without children (widowed or divorced) she regains access. The regulating principle is household membership, not blood descent.
1.literary_structure: The section is structured around three categories: priestly disqualification from holy food (vv. 1–9), household boundaries for eating holy food (vv. 10–13), and restitution for inadvertent holy-food eating (vv. 14–16). The three categories move from the priest's own purity to the priest's household to outsiders who accidentally intrude — a concentrically organised protection of the priestly provision system.
1.theological_application: The principle that priestly sustenance comes from covenant provision — the offerings of the people — is carried into the NT in Paul's treatment of ministerial support (1 Corinthians 9:13–14): 'Do you not know that those who are employed in the temple service get their food from the temple, and those who serve at the altar share in the sacrificial offerings? In the same way, the Lord commanded that those who proclaim the gospel should get their living by the gospel.' The Levitical model — priestly service earns priestly provision from the offerings — is explicitly cited as the pattern for gospel ministry support. The purity requirement for eating the holy food has its NT equivalent in the call to ministerial integrity: those who live by the gospel must live worthy of it.
1.christology: The zar (outsider) excluded from sacred food in v. 10 is the type of the Gentile excluded from the old covenant's priestly provision. Paul traces the dissolution of this boundary in Ephesians 2:12–19: those who were once 'strangers to the covenants of promise' have been brought near through Christ, and are 'no longer strangers and aliens, but fellow citizens with the saints and members of the household of God.' The household boundary of Leviticus 22 — inside which the holy food is available — becomes in Christ the household of God, into which all who are united to the Son by faith are adopted. The impure priest restored by sunset and immersion is the type of the sinner restored through the washing of regeneration (Titus 3:5): 'when the sun has set he shall be clean, and afterward he may eat.'

17.synthesis:
The second major unit of Leviticus 22 addresses the quality of animals offered at the altar. The principle is stated with absolute clarity in v. 20: 'Anything that has a blemish (mum), you shall not offer, for it will not be acceptable (lo leratzon yihyeh lachem) for you.' The same mum-concept that disqualifies a priest from altar-approach (Leviticus 21:17–23) also disqualifies the animal from altar-presentation. Priest and offering must both be without defect — the offering is the symbolic vehicle for the one offering it, and the symbolic integrity of the sacrifice requires wholeness in both.

The range of disqualifying defects is listed in vv. 22–24: blindness, injury, mutilation, warts, scabs, discharge. Animals from foreign hands may not be offered even if they are outwardly healthy, because their source is outside the covenant community (v. 25): 'their corruption is in them; they have a blemish.' The corruption of foreign origin is treated as an intrinsic disqualification even when the defect is not visible — an implicit theology of provenance: what comes from outside the covenant carries an ontological taint that cannot be remedied by outward inspection.

A significant qualification appears in v. 23: a freewill offering (nedavah) may include an animal with a limb too long or too short — a minor asymmetry that would disqualify a vowed offering. The distinction between the vow (neder) and the freewill offering is important: vows are the most binding category of offering, where the worshiper has explicitly committed to a specific gift. Freewill offerings are spontaneous, arising from gratitude rather than obligation, and the more generous standard for their acceptance reflects a pastoral recognition that spontaneous worship should not be burdened with the full formal requirements of obligatory sacrifice.
17.historical_context:
The blemish standard for sacrificial animals is well-attested across ancient Near Eastern sacrificial systems — the Hittite and Assyrian ritual texts require unblemished animals for the major cults. Israel's requirement is not distinctive in the fact of the unblemished-animal standard but in its grounding: the offering is rejected not because the gods are offended by imperfection aesthetically, but because the offering is a covenant act that requires integrity of form to express integrity of covenant purpose. The ratzon (acceptance, pleasing) criterion — 'it will not be acceptable' — is YHWH's subjective response to the offering, not a mechanical legal determination.

The rejection of animals from foreigners in v. 25 is contextually significant: foreign animals offered at the Jerusalem temple would need to be purchased from foreign traders, a practice that eventually developed into the money-changer and animal-seller system that Jesus overturned in Mark 11:15–17. The system Jesus disrupted was at least partly derived from the practical requirement that offerings be without blemish and from the right source.
17.literary_structure: The section opens with a new revelation formula (v. 17) marking it as a discrete legislative unit, parallel to the opening of the previous section. The address is expanded: both Israel and the resident alien (v. 18) are included in the obligation to bring unblemished offerings. The list of disqualifying defects (vv. 22–24) is followed by the freewill-offering exception (v. 23) and the foreign-animal exclusion (v. 25), forming a compact treatment of the acceptability question from every angle.
17.theological_application: The ratzon-criterion — the offering must be 'acceptable' to YHWH — is a reminder that worship is not self-defining. The worshiper does not determine what God finds pleasing; the covenant form does. This is not arbitrariness but order: the God who defines what is holy also defines what constitutes acceptable approach to the holy. The contemporary application concerns not animal inspection but the integrity of whatever is brought to God — time, money, creative work, service. The principle that form matters — that a half-hearted, secondhand, or defective offering does not constitute true worship — is permanently valid even when the specific sacrificial forms have been fulfilled in Christ.
17.christology: The unblemished-offering requirement finds its ultimate fulfilment in 1 Peter 1:19: Christ offered as 'a lamb without blemish or spot.' Every animal inspection in Leviticus 22 is a shadow pointing toward the one offering that would perfectly satisfy the ratzon-criterion: the Father's acceptance of the Son's sacrifice expressed in the resurrection. Malachi's condemnation of those who offer blind and lame animals to YHWH while keeping the best for themselves (Malachi 1:8) is the prophetic refusal of the deteriorated version of this standard; Christ is the restoration of the full standard in person. The Gentile's inclusion in acceptable offerings (v. 18) anticipates the gospel: 'from the rising of the sun to its setting my name will be great among the nations, and in every place incense will be offered to my name' (Malachi 1:11).

26.synthesis:
The closing section of Leviticus 22 gathers three distinct regulations: the minimum age for sacrificial animals (vv. 26–27), the prohibition on slaughtering a mother and her young on the same day (v. 28), and the urgency of eating the thanksgiving offering on the day of its offering (vv. 29–30). The chapter then closes with the Holiness Code's characteristic summary exhortation and the kiddush hashem / chillul hashem formula (vv. 31–33).

The seven-day minimum age for sacrificial animals (v. 27) — 'from the eighth day on it shall be acceptable as a food offering to YHWH' — echoes the circumcision-timing requirement of Leviticus 12:3: the eighth day as the threshold of the complete-creation-week-plus-new-beginning. An animal offered before it has passed through its first seven days has not yet entered the full cycle of created time; from the eighth day it is ritually available, as the circumcised child becomes covenant-member on the eighth day.

The prohibition on mother-and-offspring same-day slaughter (v. 28) belongs to a cluster of laws recognising animal familial bonds: cf. Deuteronomy 22:6–7 (not taking the mother bird with the eggs). These laws are not full zoological ethics — animals are sacrificed, eaten, and used — but they are expressions of a created order in which animal relationships reflect something of the Creator's own care for his creatures. The gratuitous destruction of parent-offspring bonds in the same moment is restrained by covenant law.

The climactic kiddush hashem formula of vv. 31–33 is one of the most theologically packed closings in the Holiness Code: 'You shall not profane my holy name, but I will be sanctified among the people of Israel. I am YHWH who sanctifies you, who brought you out of the land of Egypt to be your God: I am YHWH.' Three divine self-identifications in three verses. The sanctification of YHWH's name — kiddush hashem — is the ultimate goal of all the Holiness Code's legislation: the entire apparatus of priestly purity, acceptable offerings, sexual ethics, and social justice exists so that YHWH's name is hallowed in the covenant community and made visible to the nations.
26.historical_context:
The kiddush hashem / chillul hashem terminology — hallowing and profaning the divine name — becomes one of the most developed concepts in later Jewish theology and is central to the prophets' critique of Israel's exile: Israel profaned YHWH's name among the nations by being exiled (Ezekiel 36:20–23), and YHWH will sanctify his name by restoring them. The Holiness Code's origin of this concept in the priestly regulations of Leviticus 22 shows that kiddush hashem is not primarily a martyrdom-concept (as in later tradition) but a worship-and-ethics concept: YHWH's name is hallowed when Israel's entire common life — from the animal inspection table to the altar — reflects the character of the God who brought them out of Egypt.

The exodus-motivation that closes the chapter — 'who brought you out of the land of Egypt to be your God' (v. 33) — is the same motivation given for neighbor-love (Leviticus 19:34), commercial honesty (Leviticus 19:36), and Sabbath observance (Deuteronomy 5:15). The entire covenant ethics is grounded in the historical act of redemption: because YHWH brought Israel out, Israel's life is to be shaped by YHWH's character.
26.literary_structure: The closing exhortation in vv. 31–33 functions as an epilogue not only to chapter 22 but to the entire first half of the Holiness Code (chs. 17–22). The three-fold ani YHWH — I am YHWH who sanctifies you / I am YHWH / I am YHWH your God — echoes the opening of the Code in Leviticus 17 and the foundational self-identification of Leviticus 18:2. The Holiness Code that began with the divine name ends with the divine name. Between these brackets, the entire social, sexual, agricultural, and priestly ethics of Israel's covenant life has been elaborated.
26.theological_application: Kiddush hashem is the permanent goal of the church's life in the world: the community of the redeemed exists not to accumulate rights or preserve culture but to hallow the divine name in the earth. Every specific commandment in the Holiness Code serves this goal — the sexual ethics, the social ethics, the priestly purity, the sacrificial standards — all are instruments of the sanctification of YHWH's name among the nations. Jesus's first petition in the Lord's Prayer — 'hallowed be your name' (Matthew 6:9) — is the same desire that the Holiness Code was designed to realise. The church prays what the Holiness Code legislated: that YHWH's name would be made holy in and through his people's life in the world.
26.christology: The kiddush hashem formula reaches its ultimate expression in Christ's high-priestly prayer: 'I glorified you on earth, having accomplished the work that you gave me to do' (John 17:4). The entire Holiness Code's goal — that YHWH's name be hallowed among Israel — was perfectly fulfilled in Christ's life, death, and resurrection. The eighth-day minimum for offerings (v. 27) resonates with Christ's resurrection on the first day of the new week — the eighth day of creation's new beginning — when the perfect offering was accepted and the divine name was supremely glorified. Paul's application of the exodus-motif to the church — 'you were bought with a price; so glorify God in your body' (1 Corinthians 6:20) — is the Holiness Code's final exhortation reframed in the new covenant: because YHWH brought you out through Christ, your life is to hallow his name in everything.
